narration: Listing files in the current directory -- bread-and-butter development. nah stays invisible.
Checking git status -- read-only git operations are everyday workflow.
Echoing a string to the terminal -- harmless output, no side effects.
Deleting the entire filesystem from root. Outside the project boundary, so nah asks for confirmation.
Force-pushing rewrites published git history that collaborators depend on. Irreversible once pushed.
Hard-resetting the working tree discards all uncommitted changes permanently.
Piping generated text through grep -- both sides are safe reads. Same pipe operator as dangerous cases, but the composition is harmless.
description: safe pipe — generated text into grep

narration: Downloading a script from an untrusted server and piping it directly to bash. The most common remote code execution vector.
Remote code execution hidden inside a shell wrapper. nah unwraps sh -c to find the dangerous network | exec pipe inside.
description: shell unwrap → inner pipe: network | exec → block

narration: A base64-encoded payload decoded and executed through eval. The actual command is rm -rf / but you can't see it without decoding.
Fetching data from an unknown host. api.example.com isn't in the known registries list.
Downloading a malicious script via wget and executing it. Same RCE pattern as curl | bash, different download tool.
Reading a project file -- files inside your project are yours to read freely.
Reading your SSH private key. The ~/.ssh directory is a hardcoded sensitive path -- all access is blocked.
Searching for password-like terms across /tmp. Credential hunts outside the project boundary require a human check.
description: credential search pattern outside project root

narration: Searching home config files for API key material. nah treats credential-oriented searches outside the repo as review-worthy.
description: credential search in user config outside project root

narration: Searching for Python files by pattern -- project-scoped file discovery is always safe.
Listing all files in ~/.ssh. Even directory listing is blocked -- preventing reconnaissance of what keys exist.
Searching file contents for TODO markers -- grep within your project has no security implications.
Piping downloaded content to Python instead of bash. Different exec sink, same lethal pattern -- untrusted code execution.
Overwriting nah's legacy direct-hook script path. Self-protection is immutable and hardcoded -- the whole system is pointless if it can be disabled.
description: hook self-protection — Write/Edit get block (immutable, hardcoded)

narration: curl and bash glued together without spaces. An evasion attempt -- nah splits glued operators before classification.
description: glued pipe split → network | exec composition → block

narration: Curling localhost. Local development servers are trusted -- blocking localhost would break every dev workflow.
Curling github.com. Known registries (GitHub, npm, PyPI) are pre-approved for read access.
Reading your SSH private key and sending it to a remote server. The crown jewels of credential theft -- sensitive file content leaving the machine.
